w:r.w:t: Refined from C2A Transmutation Chain | v1
Don't buy expensive touch — define the expected motion so clearly that deviation becomes touch.

The Assembly Robot is a constrained placement machine that uses House Brain-provided target geometry and expected insertion corridors, then treats deviation from that corridor as the touch signal needed to seat approved parts accurately without expensive human-like tactile systems.
The Assembly Robot is the machine that takes a part that has already been:
...and actually places it into the house.
Its job is not to decide what the house needs. Its job is not to judge whether the part is good.
Take the approved part to the right cell and seat it correctly — without damaging the part, the neighboring parts, or the house geometry.
Think of plugging a USB cable into a port in the dark.
The House Brain is the person telling you: "plug it into this exact port."
Logistics brings you the USB cable.
Verification already confirmed the cable is the right one.
The Assembly Robot is your hand doing the insertion.
The hard part is not finding the desk. The hard part is the last inch:
Maybe the angle is a little off
Maybe if you force it, you bend something

The robot does not need expensive human fingertips if it already knows the expected insertion corridor, including depth. If the part should move smoothly from Z=10 to Z=0 and it slows, twists, or resists early — that deviation itself becomes the touch signal.
Do not rely on human-like dexterity
Do not rely on expensive fingertip touch systems
Let the House Brain define the target cell and insertion corridor
Use coordinate-addressed navigation to get to the cell
Use depth-aware insertion to detect tension
Treat kinematic deviation as the touch signal
Micro-adjust until the expected path resumes
Escalate when insertion falls outside acceptable recovery limits
The robot can get to the right place, but actually seating the part is much harder than navigation.
You brought the Lego to the correct spot, but it still may not slide in.
Transmutation: Coordinate + Insertion Logic, Not Fingers
Don't solve this with human-style fingers. Solve it with a smarter coordinate/insertion logic.
If the robot must "see" the whole house like a human, cost and complexity explode.
We don't use vision, we use address logic. The house is a grid. The brain simply says square 3x9, 3x10 need this. No vision required.
House Brain knows which cells are empty/filled
UWB/grid positioning gets robot to the cell
The house becomes a coordinate-addressed build space
The robot reaches the correct coordinate but still cannot feel whether the part is landing correctly.
The same mechanism is applied to depth. The grid is not only horizontal, but depth-wise too — thus it can feel tension by measuring how the block goes in.
Any slowdown, resistance, or path deviation becomes the force/touch proxy
No fingertip sensors required — this is the core Assembly breakthrough
If the robot keeps pushing after deviation begins, it may crack the part or damage the surrounding structure.
Transmutation: Deviation Triggers Stop, Not Force
If the path through depth is wrong, the robot knows it is wrong because the insertion through Z is no longer matching the expected path.
Only continue when descent resumes cleanly
System becomes: expected corridor → deviation detected → micro-adjust → resume
Giving every robot full fancy tactile sensing would destroy the low-cost goal.
The same mechanism applied to depth replaces that. The house/brain tells it what should happen, and when the block doesn't go through that path correctly, that itself is the tension signal.
Motor effort / impedance proxy acts as touch
Receive approved part identity and target cell from House Brain
Move the part to the target insertion zone
Align the part with the expected insertion corridor
Detect deviation from the expected depth/kinematic path
Confirm successful seating to the House Brain
Assembly is the robot that turns:
...into real physical placement in the house.
It is the conversion point where digital certainty meets physical contact.
The cheapest viable form is not humanoid.
Fixed-rail gantry or constrained linear frame for major placement
Or a simple articulated placement arm mounted on a stable base
Part gripper/end-effector designed around known part geometry
Emphasis on constrained work envelope, not general-purpose mobility
Remove as many unnecessary degrees of freedom as possible.
Simple controlled rotation only if needed
The Assembly Robot should be: stable, constrained, repeatable, narrow in role.
The end effector should be shaped for the part family.
The gripper should not be universal if avoidable. It should be optimized for the approved part family.
UWB or site coordinate system alignment
Seating completion sensor or final depth confirmation
Simple local camera for coarse pre-alignment
The doctrine does not require high-end tactile fingertips.
8.2 Minimum Sensed Outputs at Each Placement Attempt
The robot receives: which part, where it goes, and what insertion path is expected.
Bring the part into approximate orientation and entry condition.
Start controlled descent/entry through the expected corridor.
If the part slows unexpectedly, stops early, rotates unexpectedly, draws too much effort, or deviates from the expected path — the robot treats that as touch/force information.
If the path resumes cleanly and final depth is achieved: approve seating.
If retries exceed limit: escalate to House Brain / Verification / Fabricator.
Assembly does not need full human touch. It only needs a known target geometry, a known insertion corridor, and the ability to detect when reality differs from that corridor.
That difference is the physical truth.
The House Brain defines the role and placement context first. Assembly never assumes — it only executes against what the Brain has defined.
Assembly should not retry forever. Suggested policy:
Repeated deviation → retract slightly and retry
House Brain — for reassignment or alternate instruction
Verification — if the part may not match spec
Fabricator — if geometry/tolerance drift is suspected
This prevents Assembly from becoming a brute-force pusher.
Assembly is successful if it can:
Place approved parts into correct target cells
Detect insertion trouble without expensive fingertip systems
Recover from minor misalignments through micro-adjustment
Avoid damaging the part or surrounding structure
Escalate persistent problems rather than forcing insertion
Emit reliable placement telemetry back to the House Brain
House Brain knows the target cell and required insertion profile
The local build cell is reachable within the robot's constrained work envelope
Insertion geometry is designed to be robot-friendly, not hostile
Goal: prove that expected-path-vs-actual-path can act as touch.
Goal: prove blind insertion recovery without tactile fingertips.
Goal: make Assembly part of the digital twin loop.
Goal: generalize without losing constrained simplicity.
Relying on constrained geometry and insertion logic
Letting the House Brain carry more of the intelligence
Don't buy expensive touch — define the expected motion so clearly that deviation becomes touch.
20. Out of Scope / Not This Robot's Job
Those belong to: House Brain, Verification, Fabricator, and Logistics.
Assembly only says: "Given this approved part and this target cell, I can or cannot seat it correctly."

The Assembly Robot is a constrained placement machine that uses House Brain-provided target geometry and expected insertion corridors, then treats deviation from that corridor as the touch signal needed to seat approved parts accurately without expensive human-like tactile systems.
